claims.claim: We evaluate one-shot logprob-margin gating as a deployment safeguard for cheap-model inference, comparing it against a 3× repeated-sampling baseline on 200 rows of GSM8K across two prompt families (arithmetic-structure and question-shape).
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The logprob-margin gate accepts 20.5% of rows as cheap at a 0.5 percentage-point accuracy loss relative to all-promoted inference, while achieving a 6.3× wall-clock speedup over the 3× repeated-sampling fallback.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: However, the repeated-sampling baseline accepts roughly twice as many cheap rows (≈39.0–39.5%) with exact static-baseline recovery.

claims.claim: The result is positive but narrow: one-shot logprob-margin gating is a viable latency-reduction safeguard when a small accuracy loss and limited cheap-acceptance rate are acceptable, but it does not dominate repeated sampling when exact accuracy recovery and maximum cheap acceptance are required.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Confidence in this finding is medium, bounded by the single dataset, single model profile, and 200-row evaluation scope.

claims.claim: Deploying language models under latency and compute constraints often involves routing inputs between a cheap (fast, small) model and a more capable (slow, large) model.

evidence_refs.quote: We evaluate one-shot logprob-margin gating as a deployment safeguard for cheap-model inference, comparing it against a 3× repeated-sampling baseline on 200 rows of GSM8K across two prompt families (arithmetic-structure and question-shape). The logprob-margin gate accepts 20.5% of rows as cheap at a 0.5 percentage-point accuracy loss relative to all-promoted inference, while achieving a 6.3× wall-clock speedup over the 3× repeated-sampling fallback. claims.claim: A safeguard gate decides which inputs can safely be served by the cheap model without unacceptable accuracy loss.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Repeated-sampling gates can recover the full promoted-model baseline but require multiple forward passes per input, incurring latency proportional to the sample count.

claims.claim: This work benchmarks one-shot logprob-margin gating against a 3× repeated-sampling reference on GSM8K rows 400–599, using Bonsai cheap-profile GGUF models served by local `llama-server` instances.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We report accuracy, cheap-acceptance fraction, recovered static-loss fraction, and wall-clock latency for both strategies across two prompt families.

claims.claim: A Python harness (`capture_logprob_margin_benchmark.py`) launches local `llama-server` instances from `/tmp/enoch_llama_services`, verifies model availability via the `/models` endpoint, and issues one deterministic cheap completion per row with `n_probs=10` to capture the top-10 token logprobs at each position.
